responses.content: Sometimes we need more than one person to make a difference. in Freedoms Daughters we learn that Barbara didn't just go on a strike by herself but had a large number of people to strike with her they stood up for their equal rights and decided to make a difference together. In paragraph five she brought five other students and mentions how they've been asking for the new building and still haven't got any building, she wants to take action and strike knowing that might cause trouble.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a clear topic sentence and one specific piece of evidence from the text (Barbara bringing five other people to strike with her and wanting to take action). However, it does not include a second piece of evidence or an explanation for a second piece of evidence. The explanation for the first evidence is present (they stood up for their equal rights and decided to make a difference together). Therefore, it fits the criteria for 'partially-correct-high' as it is missing one required element for a fully correct answer.

questions.question: Review your complete comparative essay one final time. Make any last edits to strengthen your analysis, improve your evidence, or clarify your comparisons before submitting for your final grade. Make these edits in the Final Draft box.

responses.content: In Freedom Walkers and  Freedom’s daughters both main characters fought for their constitutional rights but, one of them stood up by herself and the other strikes with her school. Claudette Colvin from freedom walkers being fifteen years old and  Barbara Johns being  sixteen years old both fought for their equal rights in different ways, one went on a strike for a better school Barbara, and the other refused to give up a seat (bus) they both fought for what they new was right.Sometimes we have to stand up to what we think is right even if they tell us not to do so. In Freedom Walkers we learn that we shouldn't just normalize stuff that we know is wrong, and that we should never give the important things, like our freedom. In the very first sentence she says "its my constitutional right" which means she's defending her rights even though other people got arrested for it.Sometimes we need more than one person to make a difference. in Freedoms Daughters we learn that Barbara didn't just go on a strike by herself but had a large number of people to strike with her they stood up for their equal rights and decided to make a difference together. In paragraph five she brought five other students and mentions how they've been asking for the new building and still haven't got any building, she wants to take action and strike knowing that might cause trouble.In summary we learn that In both stories we should always take action for what we know is right even if there are consequences and that sometimes we do need more than one person to make a difference.
responses.bucket_rationale: The paragraph contains several grammar and punctuation mistakes, such as missing commas, run-on sentences, and capitalization errors. These errors go beyond mere style or phrasing suggestions.
